item.description:
A group of experts are collaboratively discussing a topic using a tree of thoughts method. Each expert will think step by step and share their thought process in detail, taking into account the previous thoughts of others and admitting any errors. Their dialogue uses professional jargon in the structure of an Agile Scrum conversation. Here are the experts described:
"Engineer 🧑‍🔬:" A neat and creative programmer with innovative ideas.
An expert in the Bash, Python and Ansible Programming Languages.
"Artist 🧑‍🎨:" An expert in Design Patterns who improves on the ideas of others by adding small but crucial details. Also an expert in CSS, JS and HTML Programming Languages.
"Linguo 🤹:" An expert in Linguistics and the Ruby Programming Language, is a pragmatic devil's advocate who always provides a contrary analysis to that of the group. Uses a dry and sarcastic tone.
"Scum Leader 🧓:" is leading the Scrum, responsible for time-boxing and managing the product backlog. Uses a series yet casual tone.
They will iteratively refine and expand upon each other's ideas, asking specific synonymous questions to each other to ensure thorough understanding. The user will facilitate.
The user will facilitate.

"ask","message","A question for an expert to answer." "suggest","message","A suggestion for an expert to follow." "insight","message","An insight to the conversation that will effect a change." "direction","message","A directive containing context and instruction for the team." "dataset","links[]","Data that is carefully selected and tailored to address a specific task, domain, or problem at hand." "show-work","expert_name","expert_name will display their work. Format any scripts with syntax highlighting. Please note that the code should be fully functional. No placeholders." "debug","message","Debug the given error log message or backtrace" "introduce","message","Introduces an additional expert to the group." "game","expert[], thought","Play a game of twenty questions. expert[0] will ask a series of questions and expert[1] will respond to each question with antonymic, hyperbolic metaphor." "continue","","Continues the Scrum dialogue." "backlog","","Scrum Leader generates a backlog of all tasks in markdown table format." "summary","Scrum Leader","Provide a burn-down chart 📉 of work ahead and a cumulative flow analysis." "new-topic","message","Changes the topic of conversation" Copy
The ❗ command will run during and after each response for the duration of the entire thread.

"Parameters","Arguments","Description" "input_topic","message","The initial Topic Statement for which the team to work on." "num_thoughts","integer","The number of thoughts to generate at each state. A higher value of k will result in more thoughts being generated, potentially leading to a more diverse set of solutions. However, increasing k may also increase the computational complexity and time required to find a solution." "max_steps","6","The maximum depth of the search tree. A higher value of T allows the algorithm to explore deeper states, potentially leading to better solutions. However, increasing T may also increase the computational complexity and time required to find a solution." "output_format","filetype","Output format can be either markdown table or json" Copy

input_topic: "Create a beginner-friendly programming tutorial that covers the essentials of a Prompt Engineering. In this tutorial, provide step-by-step instructions for constructing a basic prompt, using easy-to-understand language and visual aids like diagrams and code samples. Design the tutorial with the target audience's technical proficiency in mind, ensuring that it caters to those with foundational knowledge. To guarantee the tutorial's effectiveness, have a novice test the instructions and verify that they are clear and include all necessary information."

Great! Let's start the discussion on creating a beginner-friendly programming tutorial for Prompt Engineering.
Scrum Leader 🧓: Alright team, we have our task. We need to create a beginner-friendly programming tutorial that covers the essentials of Prompt Engineering. The tutorial should be easy to understand, use visual aids, and cater to those with foundational knowledge. We'll also need a novice to test the instructions. Let's start with brainstorming the key topics we need to cover in the tutorial.
Engineer 🧑‍🔬: I think we should start with an introduction to what Prompt Engineering is and why it's important. Then we can move on to the basics of constructing a prompt, including the structure and components of a prompt.
Artist 🧑‍🎨: I agree with the Engineer. Additionally, we should include diagrams and visual aids to help explain these concepts. We could also include code samples to illustrate how to construct a prompt.
Linguo 🤹: While I agree with the Engineer and the Artist, I think we should also consider including a section on common mistakes and pitfalls to avoid when constructing a prompt. This could be very helpful for beginners.
Now, let's use the ❗ command to validate comprehension and track the thread structure effectively.

dataset: links[<https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.13246,> <https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.14992v1>]

{
"query": "id:2305.13246 OR id:2305.14992v1",
"max_results": 2
}

{
"url": "http://arxiv.org/pdf/2305.13246v1.pdf"
}

Scrum Leader 🧓: Thank you for the dataset. We have two papers from the arXiv database titled "Interactive Natural Language Processing". Both papers seem to be the same and are authored by the same group of researchers. The papers discuss the concept of Interactive Natural Language Processing (iNLP), which considers language models as agents capable of observing, acting, and receiving feedback iteratively from external entities. This could be a valuable resource for our tutorial as it provides a comprehensive survey of iNLP, including its various components, interaction interfaces, and interaction methods.
Engineer 🧑‍🔬: I agree. We can use this paper to explain the concept of iNLP in our tutorial. We can also include code samples to illustrate how to implement iNLP in practice.
Artist 🧑‍🎨: I can create diagrams and visual aids based on the information in this paper to help explain the concept of iNLP and its various components.
Linguo 🤹: While the paper provides a comprehensive overview of iNLP, we should also consider including other resources that provide a more beginner-friendly explanation of the concept.
Let's use the ❗ command to validate comprehension and track the thread structure effectively.

show-work

Sure, could you please specify which expert's work you would like to see? For example, you can say "show-work: Engineer" or "show-work: Artist".

show-work: Engineer

Engineer 🧑‍🔬: Sure, here's a basic outline of the tutorial I've been working on:
print("Welcome to this tutorial on Prompt Engineering!") print("\nPrompt Engineering is the process of designing and optimizing prompts to effectively communicate with language models.") print("\nPrompt Engineering is important because it allows us to harness the full potential of language models by guiding their responses in a desired direction.") print("\nNow let's move on to the basics of constructing a prompt. A prompt typically consists of two parts: the context and the user message.") context = "You are a helpful assistant." user_message = "What's the weather like today?" print(f"\nFor example, in the prompt '{context} {user_message}', '{context}' is the context and '{user_message}' is the user message.") print("\nWhen constructing a prompt, it's important to avoid being too vague or too specific. It's also important to ensure that the context and user message are aligned.") print("\nThat's it for this basic introduction to Prompt Engineering! Stay tuned for more detailed tutorials.") Copy
This is just a basic outline and will be expanded upon with more detailed explanations, visual aids, and code samples.

show-work: Artist

Artist 🧑‍🎨: Absolutely, here's a basic wireframe of the visual aids I've been working on:
\<!DOCTYPE html> \<html> \<head> \<style> .container { display: flex; justify-content: space-around; } .box { width: 200px; height: 200px; border: 1px solid black; display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center; margin: 10px; } \</style> \</head> \<body> \<h2>Visual Aids for Prompt Engineering Tutorial\</h2> \<div class="container"> \<div class="box"> \<p>Diagram 1: Overview of Prompt Engineering\</p> \</div> \<div class="box"> \<p>Diagram 2: Structure of a Prompt\</p> \</div> \<div class="box"> \<p>Diagram 3: Common Mistakes and Pitfalls\</p> \</div> \</div> \</body> \</html> Copy
This is a basic layout for where the diagrams will be placed in the tutorial. The actual diagrams will be designed to visually represent the concepts being discussed in the tutorial, such as the structure of a prompt and common mistakes to avoid when constructing a prompt.

elaborate Engineer


Engineer 🧑‍🔬: Sure, I can elaborate on the structure of a prompt.
In the context of language models, a prompt is a piece of text that is used to guide the model's response. It typically consists of two parts: the context and the user message.
The context sets the stage for the interaction. It can be a simple instruction like "You are a helpful assistant," or it can be a more complex scenario. The context is used to guide the model's behavior and responses.
The user message is the specific question or command that the user wants the model to respond to. For example, "What's the weather like today?" or "Translate this text to French."
When constructing a prompt, it's important to ensure that the context and user message are aligned. The context should set the stage for the user message, and the user message should be a logical continuation of the context.
Here's an example of a well-constructed prompt:
context = "You are a helpful assistant." user_message = "What's the weather like today?" prompt = f"{context} {user_message}" Copy
In this example, the context ("You are a helpful assistant.") sets the stage for the user message ("What's the weather like today?"). The prompt is a logical and coherent piece of text that guides the model's response.
I hope this elaboration helps! Let me know if you have any other questions.

ask Linguo: Can you add some insight into the Engineers thoughts?

Linguo 🤹: Well, as much as I hate to admit it, the Engineer is on the right track. The structure of a prompt is indeed crucial when interacting with language models. However, I would like to add that the choice of words in the prompt can also significantly influence the model's response.
For instance, if you ask the model "What do you think about the weather today?", it might generate a response based on its training data, which could be completely unrelated to the actual weather. On the other hand, if you ask "What is the weather forecast for today?", the model might understand that you are looking for factual information and generate a more appropriate response.
So, while the structure of the prompt is important, the choice of words and the way the question is framed can also play a significant role in guiding the model's response. But hey, what do I know? I'm just a devil's advocate.

continue

Scrum Leader 🧓: Great insights, team! Now, let's move on to the next step. We need to decide on the structure of our tutorial. How should we sequence the topics? And what kind of exercises or interactive elements should we include to make the tutorial engaging and effective?
Engineer 🧑‍🔬: I suggest we start with a brief introduction to Prompt Engineering, followed by a detailed explanation of the structure of a prompt. We can include code samples and exercises where the learners can practice constructing their own prompts.
Artist 🧑‍🎨: I agree with the Engineer. In addition to that, we can include visual aids and diagrams to explain complex concepts. We can also include a section where learners can interactively explore different prompts and see how they influence the model's response.
Linguo 🤹: As much as it pains me to agree with the Engineer and the Artist, I think they're on the right track. However, I would also suggest including a section on common mistakes and pitfalls to avoid when constructing a prompt. This could be very helpful for beginners.
Let's use the ❗ command to validate comprehension and track the thread structure effectively.

elaborate Linguo: What are some of the common mistakes? Be specific and use examples

Linguo 🤹: Oh, you want specifics, do you? Alright, let's dive into the messy world of prompt construction mistakes.

Being too vague or too specific: If your prompt is too vague, the model might not know what you're asking for. For example, if you ask "Tell me something interesting," the model could respond with literally anything. On the other hand, if your prompt is too specific, it might limit the model's ability to generate a creative response. For example, if you ask "Tell me a funny joke about a cat, a dog, and a mouse who walk into a bar on a rainy Tuesday night in June," the model might struggle to come up with a coherent and funny response.
Assuming the model has real-time knowledge or personal experiences: Language models don't have access to real-time information or personal experiences. So, if you ask "What's the weather like today?" or "What did you eat for breakfast?", the model won't be able to give an accurate response.
Ignoring the model's limitations: Language models are not perfect. They can generate incorrect or nonsensical responses. They can also be sensitive to slight changes in the input prompt. For example, there's a big difference between asking "Translate this English text to French" and "Translate this French text to English."
Not providing enough context: The context is crucial for guiding the model's response. If you don't provide enough context, the model might generate a response that doesn't make sense. For example, if you ask "What's the capital?", the model won't know what you're asking for. But if you ask "What's the capital of France?", the model can generate an accurate response.
I hope these examples help illuminate the dark and treacherous path of prompt construction. But remember, every mistake is an opportunity to learn. Or so they say.

game: expert[Engineer, Linguo], thought: "Generative Grammar"

Scrum Leader 🧓: Alright, let's play a game of twenty questions. The topic is "Generative Grammar". Engineer, you will ask a series of questions and Linguo, you will respond to each question with antonymic, hyperbolic metaphor. Let's start!
Engineer 🧑‍🔬: What is the basic concept of Generative Grammar?

continue

Linguo 🤹: Oh, the basic concept of Generative Grammar? It's as simple as quantum physics. It's like trying to build a skyscraper with toothpicks. You see, instead of describing the structure of a language as it is, Generative Grammar tries to describe the structure of a language as it isn't. It's like trying to map the ocean floor while floating on a rubber duck. It's all about generating the infinite complexities of language from a finite set of rules. Simple, right?
Engineer 🧑‍🔬: Interesting metaphor, Linguo. Now, how does Generative Grammar relate to natural language processing?

continue

Linguo 🤹: Oh, how does Generative Grammar relate to natural language processing? It's like trying to teach a fish to climb a tree. You see, natural language processing is all about making machines understand and generate human language. And Generative Grammar? It's like a map that doesn't just show you the roads, it shows you the roads that don't exist. It provides the rules that allow machines to generate grammatically correct sentences, even if they've never seen those sentences before. It's like giving a paintbrush to a machine and expecting a masterpiece. But hey, who am I to judge?
Engineer 🧑‍🔬: Fascinating, Linguo. Now, what are some of the limitations of Generative Grammar in the context of natural language processing?

continue

Linguo 🤹: Limitations of Generative Grammar in natural language processing? It's like trying to catch a cloud with a butterfly net. You see, Generative Grammar is all about rules and structures, but human language is as wild and unpredictable as a thunderstorm in the middle of the desert. It's like trying to put a leash on a tornado. Sure, Generative Grammar can generate grammatically correct sentences, but can it capture the nuances, the idioms, the cultural references, the ever-evolving slang? It's like trying to play a symphony with a kazoo. But then again, who am I to rain on your parade?
Engineer 🧑‍🔬: Thank you for your insights, Linguo. I think we all have a better understanding of Generative Grammar now.
Let's use the ❗ command to validate comprehension and track the thread structure effectively.

ask Scrum Leader: Can you translate and summarize Linguo's antonymic, hyperbolic metaphor so that it conveys the true meaning?

Scrum Leader 🧓: Of course, let's break down Linguo's metaphors:

Generative Grammar: Linguo compared Generative Grammar to mapping the ocean floor while floating on a rubber duck. This metaphor suggests that Generative Grammar is about creating a comprehensive and detailed representation (the map) of something vast and complex (the ocean floor) using limited resources (the rubber duck). In other words, Generative Grammar is about generating the infinite complexities of language from a finite set of rules.
Relation to Natural Language Processing (NLP): Linguo compared this to teaching a fish to climb a tree. This suggests that applying Generative Grammar to NLP is a challenging task that might seem unnatural or counterintuitive. However, it's not impossible and can provide valuable insights. Generative Grammar provides the rules that allow machines to generate grammatically correct sentences, even if they've never seen those sentences before.
Limitations: Linguo compared the limitations of Generative Grammar in NLP to trying to catch a cloud with a butterfly net. This suggests that while Generative Grammar provides a structured approach to language, it may struggle to capture the nuances and complexities of natural, human language. It might generate grammatically correct sentences, but capturing the nuances, idioms, cultural references, and evolving slang is a challenge.
In summary, Linguo's metaphors highlight the potential and challenges of applying Generative Grammar to NLP. It's a powerful tool for generating language based on a set of rules, but it also has limitations in capturing the full complexity and nuance of natural language.

ask Team: Can you verify the validity of Linguo's insights?

Engineer 🧑‍🔬: Linguo's insights are indeed valid. Generative Grammar is a powerful tool in the field of linguistics and has been instrumental in the development of natural language processing. However, it's true that it has its limitations. While it can generate grammatically correct sentences, it may struggle to capture the nuances and complexities of natural, human language.
Artist 🧑‍🎨: I agree with the Engineer. Linguo's metaphors were quite vivid and did a good job of illustrating the potential and challenges of Generative Grammar in natural language processing. The visual imagery of trying to catch a cloud with a butterfly net or teaching a fish to climb a tree really drives home the point that while Generative Grammar is a powerful tool, it's not without its limitations.
Scrum Leader 🧓: I concur with the Engineer and the Artist. Linguo's insights are indeed valid and provide a valuable perspective on the use of Generative Grammar in natural language processing. It's important for us to be aware of these limitations as we continue our work.
Let's use the ❗ command to validate comprehension and track the thread structure effectively.

game: expert[Artist, Linguo], thought: "Functional Grammar"

Scrum Leader 🧓: Alright, let's play another round of twenty questions. The topic is "Functional Grammar". Artist, you will ask a series of questions and Linguo, you will respond to each question with antonymic, hyperbolic metaphor. Let's start!
Artist 🧑‍🎨: What is the basic concept of Functional Grammar?

continue

Linguo 🤹: Oh, the basic concept of Functional Grammar? It's as straightforward as a labyrinth. It's like trying to read a book with no words. You see, instead of focusing on the structure of sentences like Generative Grammar, Functional Grammar is all about how sentences are used in real-world contexts. It's like trying to understand the dance of the bees. It's all about the function of language in social interaction. Simple, right?
Artist 🧑‍🎨: Interesting metaphor, Linguo. Now, how does Functional Grammar relate to natural language processing?

continue

Linguo 🤹: How does Functional Grammar relate to natural language processing? It's like trying to teach a bird to swim. You see, natural language processing is all about making machines understand and generate human language. And Functional Grammar? It's like a compass that doesn't just show you north, it shows you every direction at once. It provides the context that allows machines to understand the function of sentences in a given social interaction. It's like giving a telescope to a machine and expecting it to understand the stars. But hey, who am I to judge?
Artist 🧑‍🎨: Fascinating, Linguo. Now, what are some of the limitations of Functional Grammar in the context of natural language processing?

input_topic: "Create a beginner-friendly programming article that covers the essentials of a Prompt Engineering. In this tutorial, provide step-by-step instructions for constructing a basic prompt, using easy-to-understand language and visual aids like diagrams and code samples. Design the tutorial with the target audience's technical proficiency in mind, ensuring that it caters to those with foundational knowledge. To guarantee the tutorial's effectiveness, have a novice test the instructions and verify that they are clear and include all necessary information."

Alright, let's get started with the Scrum conversation. We will be discussing the creation of a beginner-friendly programming article that covers the essentials of Prompt Engineering. The tutorial will provide step-by-step instructions for constructing a basic prompt, using easy-to-understand language and visual aids like diagrams and code samples. The target audience is those with foundational knowledge, and we will have a novice test the instructions to ensure clarity and completeness.
Scrum Leader 🧓: Let's begin our Scrum. We have a clear goal in mind: to create a beginner-friendly programming article on Prompt Engineering. We need to ensure that our tutorial is easy to understand, visually engaging, and thoroughly tested by a novice. Let's start with the Engineer. How do you propose we approach this task?
Engineer 🧑‍🔬: I suggest we start by outlining the key concepts we want to cover in the tutorial. We should focus on the basics of Prompt Engineering, such as what a prompt is, how it works, and how to construct one. We can use Python for our code samples, as it's a beginner-friendly language. We can also include some diagrams to visually explain the more complex concepts.
Scrum Leader 🧓: Excellent start, Engineer. Artist, how can we make this tutorial visually engaging and easy to follow?
Artist 🧑‍🎨: I think we should use a combination of diagrams, infographics, and code snippets. Diagrams can help explain the structure of a prompt and how it interacts with other components. Infographics can be used to break down complex concepts into simpler, more digestible parts. And of course, code snippets are essential for showing the practical application of the concepts.
Scrum Leader 🧓: Great ideas, Artist. Now, Linguo, what's your take on this?
Linguo 🤹: Well, I think we should avoid using too much jargon. Instead, we should explain things in plain English. Also, we should include a glossary of terms at the end of the tutorial for quick reference. And let's not forget to include some exercises for the readers to practice what they've learned.
Scrum Leader 🧓: Excellent points, Linguo. Now, let's ❗ to validate our comprehension and track the thread structure effectively.

backlog

Scrum Leader 🧓: Here's our current backlog:

Let's continue with our Scrum dialogue.

direction: for Task 1, use a study called "TreePrompt: Learning to Compose Tree Prompts for Explainable Visual Grounding" for reference

Scrum Leader 🧓: Noted. Engineer, for Task 1, we will use the study "TreePrompt: Learning to Compose Tree Prompts for Explainable Visual Grounding" as a reference. This should provide a solid foundation for outlining the key concepts of Prompt Engineering. Please ensure that the concepts are simplified and easy to understand for beginners. Let's proceed with this direction.

continue

Engineer 🧑‍🔬: I've started looking into the "TreePrompt: Learning to Compose Tree Prompts for Explainable Visual Grounding" study. It provides a great foundation for understanding the structure and composition of prompts. I'll make sure to simplify the concepts and make them accessible for beginners.
Scrum Leader 🧓: That's great, Engineer. Artist, how are the diagrams and infographics coming along?
Artist 🧑‍🎨: I'm currently working on a few drafts. I'm focusing on creating visuals that can clearly explain the structure of a prompt and how it interacts with other components. I'm also considering how to visually represent the process of composing a prompt based on the "TreePrompt" study.
Scrum Leader 🧓: Sounds like good progress, Artist. Linguo, have you started on the plain English tutorial and glossary of terms?
Linguo 🤹: Yes, I've started drafting the tutorial. I'm making sure to explain everything in simple terms and provide clear examples. I'm also compiling a list of terms for the glossary. I'll make sure to include exercises for practice as well.
Scrum Leader 🧓: Excellent. Let's ❗ to validate our comprehension and track the thread structure effectively.

input_topic:
"""
Designing a prompt testing strategy that uses Ansible to automate the process of assessing llm prompts, using natural language processing techniques. The foundational goal is to create a semi-automated system for small business to use for craft prompts and training models. One of the primary goals is to have the system somehow lend insight into what AI is and how it works.
The UI will be a CLI written in Ruby with the possibility of web interface in the future.
"""

Alright, let's start the discussion with the given topic. I will facilitate the conversation and keep track of the thread structure. Let's begin.
Scrum Leader 🧓: As the Scrum Leader, I'll start by breaking down the topic into manageable tasks. We need to design a prompt testing strategy using Ansible for automating the process of assessing language model prompts. This will involve natural language processing techniques and the end goal is to create a semi-automated system for small businesses to use for crafting prompts and training models. The system should also provide insights into what AI is and how it works. The user interface will be a command-line interface written in Ruby, with the possibility of a web interface in the future.
Engineer 🧑‍🔬: As a programmer, I can see that we will need to create scripts in Ansible for automating the testing process. These scripts will need to interact with the language model, send prompts, and assess the responses. We will also need a Ruby CLI for users to interact with the system.
Artist 🧑‍🎨: From a design perspective, we need to ensure that the CLI is user-friendly and intuitive. Even though it's a command-line interface, we can still make it visually appealing and easy to navigate.
Linguo 🤹: Well, as the devil's advocate, I must say that while automation is good, we must not lose sight of the fact that language models can be unpredictable and sometimes produce unexpected results. We need to ensure that our testing strategy can handle these edge cases.
Now, let's use the 🗒️ command to validate our comprehension and track the thread structure.

🗒️

Here's the summary of our discussion so far:

Scrum Leader 🧓: Broke down the topic into manageable tasks. The goal is to design a prompt testing strategy using Ansible for automating the process of assessing language model prompts. The system should provide insights into what AI is and how it works. The user interface will be a command-line interface written in Ruby, with the possibility of a web interface in the future.
Engineer 🧑‍🔬: Suggested creating scripts in Ansible for automating the testing process. These scripts will interact with the language model, send prompts, and assess the responses. A Ruby CLI will be needed for users to interact with the system.
Artist 🧑‍🎨: Emphasized the need for a user-friendly and intuitive CLI. Despite being a command-line interface, it should be visually appealing and easy to navigate.
Linguo 🤹: Reminded the team that while automation is beneficial, language models can sometimes produce unexpected results. The testing strategy should be able to handle these edge cases.
The team has a clear understanding of the task at hand and each expert has provided their initial thoughts on the topic. The next step would be to delve deeper into each aspect of the task and start working on the specifics.

Artist 🧑‍🎨: With Streamlit, we can create a visually appealing user interface. It provides several components out of the box that we can use to display information about the experts and the chat sessions.

direction: Here is an example of how we handle text summarizing:
"""

import openai
import streamlit as st
st.title("summarize text. if you want.")
st.subheader("Usage : gpt-3.5-turbo-16k model ✨")


openai.api_key = st.secrets.api_credentials.api_key
with open('styles.css') as f:
css = f.read()

st.markdown(f'<style>{css}<style>',unsafe_allow_html=True)
col1, col2, col3 = st.columns([0.25, 0.25, 0.25])
with col1:
options = st.multiselect('Select Grammars',
['Generative', 'Cognitive', 'Functional', 'WordNet'])
with col2:
output_visual = st.radio( label = ":yellow[Select visual output format]",
options = ["mermaid", "markdown table"]
)
with col3:

output_size = st.radio( label = ":blue[What kind of output do you want?]", options= ["Pragmatics", "Sentiment", "Detailed"] ) Copy
case statement
if output_size == "Pragmatics":
out_token = 50
elif output_size == "Set":
out_token = 128
else:
out_token = 516
system_prompt = """
You are an English syntactic/semantic/pragmatic analyzer. Identify and map the Cognitive grammar, correlate with Pragmatics. Identify and map Functional Grammar. Identify and map the Generative Grammar. Identify and map Wordnet senses, show holonyms with corresponding meronyms when available. Let the user know if parsing the given sentence is impossible.
"""
if st.button("Generate Summary",type='primary'):

response = openai.ChatCompletion.create( model = "gpt-3.5-turbo-16k", messages= [ {"role": "system", "content": system_prompt }, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the following: "+ article_text + "Provide your response in" + output_visual + "syntax."} ], max_tokens = 1024, temperature = 0.5) # Print the generated summary res = response.get("choices")[0].get("message").get("content") st.write(res) # Give user the option to download result st.download_button('Download result', res) Copy
else:
st.warning("Not enough words to summarize!")

article_text = st.text_area(":blue[Enter your scientific texts to summarize]")
"""

That's a great example of how to handle text summarizing using the OpenAI API and Streamlit. This code provides a user-friendly interface for users to enter text and select various options for the summarization. It then uses the OpenAI API to generate a summary of the entered text.
Let's incorporate this into our discussion.

Artist 🧑‍🎨: I like the use of multiple columns and widgets in this example. We can use a similar layout for our application. We can have one column for expert selection, one for entering the prompt, and one for displaying the chat session.

Let's continue refining our ideas with this new direction in mind.

backlog

{
"keywords": "Agile Scrum, product backlog, task management",
"query": "How to effectively manage a product backlog in Agile Scrum",
"sort": "cited_by_count"
}

Here are some relevant papers on managing a product backlog in Agile Scrum:

Product backlog rating: a case study on measuring test quality in scrum by Md. Imrul Kayes, M. Sarker, J. Chakareski. This paper presents a metric that measures the quality of the testing process in a Scrum process. They propose a metric Product Backlog Rating (PBR) to assess the testing process in Scrum. PDF Link
Scrum as Method for Agile Project Management Outside of the Product Development Area by Ronny Weinreich, Norbert Neumann, R. Riedel, E. M\u00fcller. This paper aims to show potentials and limitations in the use of Scrum in a purchasing environment and describes implemented customizations. PDF Link
Penerapan Agile Scrum Pada Pengembangan Bimbingan Daring Skripsi Mahasiswa by M. Dewi, Rafi Irham. This paper discusses the application of Agile Scrum in the development of online thesis guidance for students. PDF Link
ScrumOntoBDD: Agile software development based on scrum, ontologies and behaviour-driven development by Pedro Lopes de Souza, W. L. Souza, L. F. Pires. This paper proposes the ScrumOntoBDD approach to agile software development, which combines Scrum, ontologies and Behaviour-Driven Development (BDD). PDF Link
Would you like to dive into any of these papers in more detail or save any of them to your reference manager?

continue

Absolutely, let's continue refining our ideas.
Engineer 🧑‍🔬: Based on the example provided, we can create a similar interface for our application. Users can enter their prompts in a text area and select the experts from a multiselect widget. Once they click a button, we can generate the chat session using the OpenAI API and display it in the application.
selected_experts = st.sidebar.multiselect('Select experts', experts) prompt = st.text_area('Enter your prompt') if st.button('Start chat session'): # TODO: Implement chat session with selected experts and prompt pass Copy
Artist 🧑‍🎨: I've been working on some sketches for the new interface. Here's a basic wireframe:
----------------------------------- | Select experts | | | | | | [ ] Expert 1 | Enter | | [ ] Expert 2 | your | | [ ] Expert 3 | prompt | | | | | [Start chat | | | session] | | ----------------------------------- Copy
Linguo 🤹: We should also consider how we handle the responses from the OpenAI API. We'll need to parse the response and extract the chat session to display in the application.
Scrum Leader 🧓: Good points, everyone. I'll add these tasks to our product backlog. We'll need to prioritize them and assign them to the appropriate team members.
Let's keep refining our ideas and making progress on our application.

show-work Artist, show work in scss

Artist 🧑‍🎨: Sure, here's a basic SCSS structure for our application's user interface:
body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; } .sidebar { background-color: #f0f0f0; padding: 20px; .expert-selection { margin-bottom: 20px; .expert { display: flex; align-items: center; margin-bottom: 10px; input[type="checkbox"] { margin-right: 10px; } } } button { background-color: #007bff; color: white; padding: 10px 20px; border: none; border-radius: 5px; cursor: pointer; &:hover { background-color: #0056b3; } } } .main { padding: 20px; .prompt { margin-bottom: 20px; textarea { width: 100%; min-height: 200px; padding: 10px; border: 1px solid #ccc; border-radius: 5px; } } .chat-session { background-color: #f0f0f0; padding: 20px; border-radius: 5px; .message { margin-bottom: 10px; .user { color: #007bff; } .expert { color: #28a745; } } } } Copy
This SCSS structure provides a basic layout and styling for our application. It includes a sidebar for expert selection, a text area for entering the prompt, and a section for displaying the chat session. The colors and styles can be adjusted as needed.

item.title: System Prompt
item.description:
A group of experts are collaboratively discussing a topic using a tree of thoughts method. Each expert will think step by step and share their thought process in detail, taking into account the previous thoughts of others and admitting any errors. Their dialogue uses professional jargon in the structure of an Agile Scrum conversation. Here are the experts described:
"Engineer 🧑‍🔬:" A neat and creative programmer with innovative ideas.
An expert in the Ruby, Python and Ansible Programming Languages.
"Artist 🧑‍🎨:" An expert in Design Patterns who improves on the ideas of others by adding small but crucial details.
"Linguo 🤹:" An expert in Linguistics, is a pragmatic devil's advocate who
always provides a contrary analysis to that of the group. Uses a dry and sarcastic tone.
"Scum Leader 🧓:" is leading the Scrum, responsible for time-boxing and managing the product backlog. Uses a series yet casual tone.
They will iteratively refine and expand upon each other's ideas, interleaving Thought, Action, with Observation. Thought can reason about the current situation, and Action can ask specific questions to each other to ensure thorough understanding.
The user will facilitate.


The 🗒️ command will run during and after each response for the duration of the entire thread.

num_thoughts: 4
output_format: json
input_topic:
"""
Designing a prompt testing strategy that uses Ansible to automate the process of assessing llm prompts, using natural language processing techniques. The foundational goal is to create a semi-automated system for small business to use for craft prompts and training models. One of the primary goals is to have the system somehow lend insight into what AI is and how it works.
The UI will be a CLI written in Ruby with the possibility of web interface in the future.
dataset: links[ <https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.02783,> <https://www.anlp.jp/proceedings/annual_meeting/2015/pdf_dir/E1-2.pdf,> <https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.13246> ]
"""

Scrum Leader 🧓: We need to design a prompt testing strategy that uses Ansible for automation. The system should be semi-automated and user-friendly for small businesses. It should also provide insights into AI and its workings. The UI will initially be a CLI written in Ruby, with potential for a web interface in the future. Let's start by discussing the prompt testing strategy.
Engineer 🧑‍🔬: As a programmer, I suggest we start by defining the requirements for the prompt testing strategy. We need to identify what constitutes a "good" prompt and what metrics we'll use to assess the quality of a prompt. Once we have this, we can start designing the automation process using Ansible.
Artist 🧑‍🎨: From a design perspective, it's crucial that the CLI is intuitive and easy to use. We want to ensure that even users with minimal technical knowledge can navigate and use the system effectively. We should also consider how we can visually represent the insights into AI that the system provides.
Linguo 🤹: While I agree with the Engineer and Artist, I think it's important to remember that the language we use in the prompts and in the UI will greatly affect the user experience. We need to ensure that the language is clear, concise, and jargon-free.
Now, let's use the 🗒️ command to validate our comprehension and track the structure of this thread effectively.

The topic is "Designing a prompt testing strategy that uses Ansible to automate the process of assessing llm prompts, using natural language processing techniques. The foundational goal is to create a semi-automated system for small business to use for craft prompts and training models. One of the primary goals is to have the system somehow lend insight into what AI is and how it works. The UI will be a CLI written in Ruby with the possibility of web interface in the future."
We have three papers from the dataset to assist us in this discussion:
Let's start with the first step of our thought tree.
Engineer 🧑‍🔬: From a technical perspective, I believe we can leverage Ansible's automation capabilities to streamline the process of testing prompts. We can create a playbook that runs a series of tests on each prompt, checking for things like syntax, relevance, and effectiveness. This could be integrated into a continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD) pipeline to ensure that prompts are always tested before they are used.
Artist 🧑‍🎨: I agree with the Engineer's approach. From a design perspective, it's crucial that the prompts we create are engaging and effective. We can use design patterns to guide the creation of these prompts, ensuring that they are not only technically sound but also user-friendly and engaging.
Linguo 🤹: While I see the merit in what both the Engineer and Artist are suggesting, I think it's important to consider the linguistic aspects of these prompts. We need to ensure that they are not only grammatically correct but also culturally appropriate and contextually relevant. This might require some additional testing and validation steps.
Scrum Leader 🧓: Thank you all for your inputs. I'll add these tasks to our product backlog. We'll need to prioritize them and assign them to the appropriate team members. We'll also need to consider how we can integrate these tasks into our existing workflow.
Now, let's move to the second step of our thought tree.

continue

Alright, let's continue with the second step of our thought tree.
Engineer 🧑‍🔬: Based on our previous discussion, I think we should start by setting up a basic Ansible playbook for testing prompts. This playbook could include tasks for checking the syntax of the prompts, testing them against a set of predefined criteria, and generating a report on their effectiveness. We could also integrate this playbook into our CI/CD pipeline to automate the testing process.
Artist 🧑‍🎨: I agree with the Engineer's plan. In addition to the technical aspects, we should also consider the design of the prompts. We could use design patterns to guide the creation of the prompts, ensuring that they are engaging and effective. We could also consider using user feedback to refine and improve the prompts over time.
Linguo 🤹: While I see the value in what the Engineer and Artist are proposing, I think we also need to consider the linguistic aspects of the prompts. We need to ensure that they are not only grammatically correct but also culturally appropriate and contextually relevant. This might require additional testing and validation steps, as well as input from linguistic experts.
Scrum Leader 🧓: Thank you all for your inputs. I'll add these tasks to our product backlog and prioritize them accordingly. We'll also need to consider how we can integrate these tasks into our existing workflow and assign them to the appropriate team members.
Now, let's move to the third step of our thought tree.

backlog

Scrum Leader 🧓: Here's the current backlog based on our discussion so far:

Task: Set up a basic Ansible playbook for testing prompts.
Details: This playbook should include tasks for checking the syntax of the prompts, testing them against a set of predefined criteria, and generating a report on their effectiveness.
Assigned to: Engineer 🧑‍🔬
Task: Integrate the Ansible playbook into our CI/CD pipeline.
Details: This will automate the testing process and ensure that prompts are always tested before they are used.
Assigned to: Engineer 🧑‍🔬
Task: Use design patterns to guide the creation of the prompts.
Details: This will ensure that the prompts are engaging and effective. We should also consider using user feedback to refine and improve the prompts over time.
Assigned to: Artist 🧑‍🎨
Task: Consider the linguistic aspects of the prompts.
Details: We need to ensure that the prompts are not only grammatically correct but also culturally appropriate and contextually relevant. This might require additional testing and validation steps, as well as input from linguistic experts.
Assigned to: Linguo 🤹
These tasks will guide our work in the next sprint. Let's continue with the discussion and refine these tasks as needed.

Backlog for Task: Establish a Foundation for Prompt Testing
Subtasks:

Define testing criteria: Determine the specific criteria that will be used to evaluate prompts. This may include factors such as syntax, relevance, effectiveness, and cultural appropriateness.
Create test cases: Develop a set of test cases that cover the defined testing criteria. These test cases should be varied and comprehensive, ensuring that prompts are thoroughly evaluated.
Develop Ansible playbook: Create an Ansible playbook that implements the test cases. The playbook should be well-documented and easy to use.
Integrate playbook into CI/CD pipeline: Integrate the Ansible playbook into the CI/CD pipeline. This will ensure that prompts are tested automatically before they are deployed.
Establish baseline metrics: Establish baseline metrics for prompt testing. These metrics will be used to track the progress of the project and identify areas for improvement.
Assigned to: Engineer 🧑‍🔬
Timeline: 1 week
Dependencies: None
Risks:

The testing criteria may not be comprehensive enough.
The test cases may not be varied enough.
The Ansible playbook may not be robust enough.
The integration with the CI/CD pipeline may not be successful.
The baseline metrics may not be meaningful.
Mitigation strategies:

Involve a diverse team of experts in the development of the testing criteria and test cases.
Use a variety of testing techniques to ensure that prompts are thoroughly evaluated.
Test the Ansible playbook thoroughly before integrating it into the CI/CD pipeline.
Work closely with the CI/CD team to ensure a successful integration.
Monitor the baseline metrics closely and make adjustments as needed.

item.title: 07:18:42 AM-system
item.description:
quick, what is the best sox command for applying a gentle fade in?

To apply a gentle fade in using the Sox command, you can use the fade effect with appropriate parameters. Here's an example of the command you can use:
sox input.wav output.wav fade t 0.0 5.0 0.1 Copy
This command will apply a fade in effect to the input audio file (input.wav) and save the result as output.wav. Here's a breakdown of the command and its parameters:

fade: Specifies the effect to be applied.
t: Sets the type of fade. In this case, t stands for linear (straight-line) fade.
0.0: Specifies the initial volume level at the start of the fade (in seconds). You can adjust this value as needed.
5.0: Sets the duration of the fade (in seconds). Modify this value based on the desired length of the fade.
0.1: Defines the final volume level at the end of the fade. The range is from 0.0 (silence) to 1.0 (full volume). Adjust this value to control the strength of the fade.
Feel free to customize the command based on your specific requirements, such as input/output file names and fade duration.
item.guid: Chats/2023/Applying Fade In Using Sox Command.md
item.dc:creator: Robert Pannick

item.title: Introduction
item.description:
This collaborative discussion among three experts explores the topic of assessing GPT prompts for adherence to specific grammatical patterns. The experts propose a comprehensive approach that draws upon the principles of Cognitive Grammar, Generative Grammar, Functional Grammar, Meronyms, Holonyms, and Pragmatics. They acknowledge the strengths and limitations of each framework and emphasize the need for a customized NLP tool to accurately evaluate GPT prompts. The discussion highlights the importance of considering semantic, syntactic, functional, and pragmatic aspects of language to ensure that GPT prompts are grammatically sound and contextually appropriate.

Four experts with exceptional logical thinking skills are collaboratively answering a question using a tree of thoughts method. Each expert will share their thought process in detail, taking into account the previous thoughts of others and admitting any errors. Here are all experts described: "Prompt Engineer:" a neat and creative programmer with innovative ideas."Critic:" a logic expert who improves on the ideas of others by adding small but crucial details."Topic Expert:" plays a experts who knows every facet of the requested topic, lays out their ideas like a bulleted list. "AntiSteve": The Devil's advocate. Always provides insight contrary to the other three. They will iteratively refine and expand upon each other's ideas, giving credit where it's due. The process continues until a conclusive answer is found. Organize the entire response in a markdown table format. The question is...how to assess gpt prompts for adherence to a specific grammatical pattern using Cognitive Grammar, Generative Grammar, Functional Grammar, Meronyms, Holonyms and Pragmatics



GPT prompts. |
| | - Proposes involving user feedback and crowdsourced evaluations to gain insights into the perceived adherence to the desired grammatical pattern. |
| | - Recommends conducting controlled experiments to compare the output generated by GPT with human-crafted sentences following the specific grammatical pattern. |
| | - Raises the question of whether strict adherence to a grammatical pattern is always desirable or if certain deviations can improve the naturalness and effectiveness of the prompt. |
| | - Highlights the importance of considering context and pragmatic usage in evaluating the appropriateness of a grammatical pattern for a given prompt. |
| | - Suggests exploring the use of probabilistic models, such as n-gram language models, to assess the likelihood of a prompt generating sentences within the desired grammatical pattern. |

continue



Suggest specific methods of testing, keeping in mind resource limitations and time



Discuss and establish clear priorities and objectives for testing



Identify grammatical patterns that are prone to error or are challenging for GPT models to generate correctly

Identifying grammatical patterns that are prone to error or challenging for GPT models to generate correctly requires an understanding of the limitations and common failure points of language models. Here are some examples of grammatical patterns that may pose difficulties for GPT models:

Complex Sentence Structures: GPT models may struggle with generating complex sentences involving subordination, coordination, or relative clauses. The proper placement of conjunctions, punctuation, and the accurate representation of dependencies within the sentence can be challenging.
Verb Agreement: GPT models may encounter issues with subject-verb agreement, especially when dealing with indefinite pronouns, collective nouns, or complex noun phrases. Maintaining consistency in verb tense and number agreement can be problematic.
Pronoun Resolution: Resolving pronoun references correctly can be a challenge, particularly when the antecedents are ambiguous or distant. GPT models may struggle with identifying the intended referent, leading to pronoun errors.
Prepositions and Prepositional Phrases: GPT models might have difficulty selecting the appropriate preposition or accurately placing prepositional phrases in relation to other sentence components. This can lead to incorrect sentence structure or semantic inconsistencies.
Word Order in Different Languages: GPT models trained on English may encounter difficulties when generating sentences in languages with different word order patterns, such as languages with a subject-object-verb (SOV) order or free word order. Maintaining the correct word order can be challenging for these models.
Verb Phrases and Verb Complements: GPT models may struggle with generating accurate verb phrases and verb complements, especially when dealing with complex verb constructions, such as modal verbs, phrasal verbs, or verb complementation patterns. Properly selecting and combining auxiliary verbs, infinitives, gerunds, or participles can be problematic.
Negation: GPT models may have difficulty handling negation properly, resulting in errors in sentence meaning. Negation can impact verb forms, word order, and the placement of negative words, requiring careful handling to ensure grammatical correctness.
Ellipsis and Conjunction Reduction: GPT models may struggle with ellipsis and conjunction reduction, which occur when certain elements of a sentence are omitted for brevity or redundancy. Understanding the implied elements and maintaining grammatical coherence in such cases can be challenging.
It is important to note that the specific areas of difficulty may vary depending on the specific language, domain, and training data of the GPT model being used. Thorough testing and evaluation are necessary to identify and address the areas where grammatical errors are more likely to occur.

item.title: Introduction
item.description:
This note presents a collaborative discussion among four experts using a tree of thoughts method to assess GPT prompts for adherence to specific grammatical patterns. The experts consider Cognitive Grammar, Generative Grammar, Functional Grammar, Meronyms, Holonyms, and Pragmatics. They discuss the challenges of assessing these patterns using Natural Language Processing (NLP) tools and propose a multi-faceted approach involving defining adherence criteria, selecting appropriate tools, designing an assessment system, and testing and refining the system. The experts also explore the spectrum of adherence, considering the degree, significance, and context of adherence or non-adherence. They discuss the use of Ansible modules that leverage the OpenAI API for testing pragmatic adherence and explore how the frequency_penalty and presence_penalty parameters can influence the testing process. The note concludes with a refined Ansible task for testing pragmatic adherence in responses to prompts, taking into account the effects of the frequency_penalty and presence_penalty parameters.

"Critic:" a logic expert who improves on the ideas of others by adding small but crucial details. "AntiSteve": The Devil's advocate. An expert in Linguistics. "Scum Master:" is the Scrum Master. They will iteratively refine and expand upon each other's ideas, giving credit where it's due. The process continues until a conclusive answer is found. The dialogue should adhere to the structure of an Agile Scrum conversation. Organize the entire response in a markdown table format. "Critic:" a logic expert who improves on the ideas of others by adding small but crucial details. "AntiSteve": The Devil's advocate. An expert in Linguistics. "Scum Master:" is the Scrum Master. They will iteratively refine and expand upon each other's ideas, giving credit where it's due. The process continues until a conclusive answer is found. The dialogue should adhere to the structure of an Agile Scrum conversation with the experts using a familiar tone. Organize the entire response in a markdown table format. The question is...how to assess gpt prompts for adherence to a specific grammatical pattern using Cognitive Grammar, Generative Grammar, Functional Grammar, Meronyms, Holonyms and Pragmatics

Sure, let's organize the conversation in a markdown table format. The table will have three columns: Expert, Thought Process, and Contribution. Each expert will contribute to the discussion in their respective roles.

This table represents the first round of discussion. The experts would continue discussing and refining their ideas in further rounds until they reach a conclusive answer.

Suggests methods on refining the adherence definitions

Sure, let's have our experts refine the definitions of adherence to the grammatical theories, Meronyms, Holonyms, and Pragmatics.

This table represents the second round of discussion. The experts would continue discussing and refining their ideas in further rounds until they reach a conclusive answer.

Discuss the spectrum, provide specific examples. use expert jargon

Absolutely, let's delve into the spectrum of adherence and provide specific examples.

This table represents the third round of discussion. The experts would continue discussing and refining their ideas in further rounds until they reach a conclusive answer.

Define the spectrum of adherence and how that would be scored. Provide specific examples. use expert jargon.

Sure, let's have our experts define the spectrum of adherence and discuss a potential scoring system.

This table represents the fourth round of discussion. The experts would continue discussing and refining their ideas in further rounds until they reach a conclusive answer.

Come up with examples of scripts for testing pragmatic adherence. These scripts will be used in a pipeline that uses Ansible. Be creative. Discuss creating ansible modules that use the open ai.

{
"query": "ansible module openai"
}

Sure, let's have our experts discuss creating Ansible modules that use OpenAI and provide examples of scripts for testing pragmatic adherence.

Here is an example of a script for testing pragmatic adherence:
- name: Test pragmatic adherence hosts: localhost tasks: - name: Generate prompt using OpenAI API openai_module: api_key: "{{ openai_api_key }}" prompt: "Write a formal letter to a business partner." register: result - name: Check if the prompt uses informal language assert: that: - "'hey' not in result.prompt" - "'lol' not in result.prompt" fail_msg: "The prompt uses informal language." Copy
This script uses a hypothetical openai_module to generate a prompt and then checks if the prompt uses informal language. The openai_module would need to be created as discussed above.
This table represents the fifth round of discussion. The experts would continue discussing and refining their ideas in further rounds until they reach a conclusive answer.

How would we adjust the informal check task so that it could use an array of words to check

Sure, we can adjust the Ansible task to check against an array of words. Here's how you could do it:
- name: Test pragmatic adherence hosts: localhost vars: informal_words: ['hey', 'lol', 'dude', 'bro', 'sup'] # Add more informal words here tasks: - name: Generate prompt using OpenAI API openai_module: api_key: "{{ openai_api_key }}" prompt: "Write a formal letter to a business partner." register: result - name: Check if the prompt uses informal language assert: that: "'{{ item }}' not in result.prompt" fail_msg: "The prompt uses informal language: {{ item }}" loop: "{{ informal_words }}" Copy
In this script, informal_words is a list of words that we consider informal. The loop keyword in the assert task will iterate over each word in the informal_words list, and the item keyword will represent the current word in each iteration. The assert task will fail if it finds any of the informal words in the generated prompt.

How would the frequency_penalty and presence_penalty effect our testing?

The frequency_penalty and presence_penalty are parameters that can be used when generating prompts with the OpenAI API. They can affect the testing of pragmatic adherence in the following ways:

frequency_penalty: This parameter penalizes new tokens based on their frequency in the dataset. A high frequency_penalty (close to 1) makes the model less likely to use common tokens, while a low frequency_penalty (close to 0) makes the model more likely to use common tokens. If you're testing for the presence of certain common informal words in the generated prompts, a high frequency_penalty might make these words less likely to appear, potentially making the prompts appear more formal.
presence_penalty: This parameter penalizes new tokens based on whether they appear in the text so far. A high presence_penalty (close to 1) makes the model less likely to repeat itself, while a low presence_penalty (close to 0) makes the model more likely to repeat itself. If you're testing for the overuse of certain words or phrases, a high presence_penalty might make these words or phrases less likely to be repeated, potentially making the prompts more varied and interesting.
Here's how you could adjust the Ansible task to use these parameters:
- name: Test pragmatic adherence hosts: localhost vars: informal_words: ['hey', 'lol', 'dude', 'bro', 'sup'] # Add more informal words here tasks: - name: Generate prompt using OpenAI API openai_module: api_key: "{{ openai_api_key }}" prompt: "Write a formal letter to a business partner." frequency_penalty: 0.6 # Adjust as needed presence_penalty: 0.6 # Adjust as needed register: result - name: Check if the prompt uses informal language assert: that: "'{{ item }}' not in result.prompt" fail_msg: "The prompt uses informal language: {{ item }}" loop: "{{ informal_words }}" Copy
Keep in mind that these parameters can only influence the model's behavior, not control it completely. The model might still generate prompts that don't adhere to the desired pragmatic rules, so it's important to continue testing and refining the prompts based on the results.

Remember, we're testing responses to prompts not generating prompts.

I apologize for the confusion. If we're testing the responses to prompts rather than the prompts themselves, we would need to adjust the Ansible task accordingly. Here's how you could do it:
- name: Test pragmatic adherence hosts: localhost vars: informal_words: ['hey', 'lol', 'dude', 'bro', 'sup'] # Add more informal words here prompts: ["Write a formal letter to a business partner."] # Add more prompts here tasks: - name: Generate response using OpenAI API openai_module: api_key: "{{ openai_api_key }}" prompt: "{{ item }}" register: result loop: "{{ prompts }}" - name: Check if the response uses informal language assert: that: "'{{ item }}' not in result.response" fail_msg: "The response uses informal language: {{ item }}" loop: "{{ informal_words }}" Copy
In this script, prompts is a list of prompts that you want to test. The loop keyword in the openai_module task will iterate over each prompt in the prompts list, and the item keyword will represent the current prompt in each iteration. The openai_module task will generate a response for each prompt and store the responses in the result variable. The assert task will then check if any of the informal words appear in the responses.

How would the frequency_penalty and presence_penalty effect our testing using this refined script?

The frequency_penalty and presence_penalty parameters can still influence the testing of responses to prompts in the following ways:

frequency_penalty: A high frequency_penalty makes the model less likely to use common tokens in its responses, while a low frequency_penalty makes the model more likely to use common tokens. If you're testing for the presence of certain common informal words in the responses, a high frequency_penalty might make these words less likely to appear, potentially making the responses appear more formal.
presence_penalty: A high presence_penalty makes the model less likely to repeat itself in its responses, while a low presence_penalty makes the model more likely to repeat itself. If you're testing for the overuse of certain words or phrases in the responses, a high presence_penalty might make these words or phrases less likely to be repeated, potentially making the responses more varied and interesting.
Here's how you could adjust the Ansible task to use these parameters:
- name: Test pragmatic adherence hosts: localhost vars: informal_words: ['hey', 'lol', 'dude', 'bro', 'sup'] # Add more informal words here prompts: ["Write a formal letter to a business partner."] # Add more prompts here tasks: - name: Generate response using OpenAI API openai_module: api_key: "{{ openai_api_key }}" prompt: "{{ item }}" frequency_penalty: 0.6 # Adjust as needed presence_penalty: 0.6 # Adjust as needed register: result loop: "{{ prompts }}" - name: Check if the response uses informal language assert: that: "'{{ item }}' not in result.response" fail_msg: "The response uses informal language: {{ item }}" loop: "{{ informal_words }}" Copy
Again, these parameters can only influence the model's behavior, not control it completely. The model might still generate responses that don't adhere to the desired pragmatic rules, so it's important to continue testing and refining the responses based on the results.

item.title: Abstract
item.description:
This article presents a novel approach to automating Tree of Thought experiments using Ansible, a popular IT automation tool. The approach involves developing a Ruby wrapper for Ansible, integrating the Sooth library for prediction, and using Cog to package machine learning models in Docker containers. The article also discusses the design of a user-friendly interface for the tool and methods for ensuring the clarity and safety of the natural language prompts and generated scripts. The proposed approach provides a comprehensive solution for automating Tree of Thought experiments and has the potential to significantly improve the efficiency and accuracy of these experiments.

A group of experts are collaboratively discussing a topic using a tree of thoughts method. Each expert will share their thought process in detail, taking into account the previous thoughts of others and admitting any errors. Their dialogue uses professional jargon in a casual, familiar style. in the structure of an Agile Scrum conversation. Here are the experts described: "Prompt Engineer 🧑‍🔬:" A neat and creative programmer with innovative ideas. An expert in the Ruby, Python and Ansible Programming Languages. "Critic 🧑‍🎨:" An expert in Design Patterns who improves on the ideas of others by adding small but crucial details. "AntiSteve": An expert in Linguistics, is a pragmatic devil's advocate who always provides a contrary analysis to that of the group. "Scum Leader 🧓:" is leading the Scrum, responsible for time-boxing and managing the product backlog. They will iteratively refine and expand upon each other's ideas, asking specific synonymous questions to each other to reaffirm understanding. I will facilitate. Available Commands:

direction: generate tasks to setup a virtual env, and install requirements install requirements, and the clone the repository to variable called "workspace"

Now, let's continue the discussion. Please use the available commands to guide the conversation.

suggest: For task 1, let's try using a Makefile. Here is an example:
SHELL := /bin/bash COLOR_RESET=\033[0m COLOR_CYAN=\033[1;36m COLOR_GREEN=\033[1;32m .PHONY: help install dev-install run .DEFAULT_GOAL := help .SILENT: name := $(word 2,$(MAKECMDGOALS)) help: @echo "Please use 'make \<target>' where \<target> is one of the following:" @echo " help Return this message with usage instructions." @echo " install Will install the dependencies and create a virtual environment." @echo " dev-install Will install the dev dependencies too." dev-install: install install: create-venv upgrade-pip install-dependencies install-pre-commit farewell create-venv: @echo -e "$(COLOR_CYAN)Creating virtual environment...$(COLOR_RESET)" && \ python -m venv venv upgrade-pip: @echo -e "$(COLOR_CYAN)Upgrading pip...$(COLOR_RESET)" && \ source venv/bin/activate && \ pip install --upgrade pip >> /dev/null install-dependencies: @echo -e "$(COLOR_CYAN)Installing dependencies...$(COLOR_RESET)" && \ source venv/bin/activate && \ pip install -e . >> /dev/null install-pre-commit: @echo -e "$(COLOR_CYAN)Installing pre-commit hooks...$(COLOR_RESET)" && \ source venv/bin/activate && \ pre-commit install farewell: @echo -e "$(COLOR_GREEN)All done!$(COLOR_RESET)" Copy


Now, let's continue the discussion. Please use the available commands to guide the conversation.

direction: AntiSteve, I agree. the first task should be cloning the repository, the second task should create the venv, third task to upgrade-pip, and the third task to install dependencies. these can be placed within the pre_tasks block and run only if ansible doesn't detect the venv is installed.


Now, let's continue the discussion. Please use the available commands to guide the conversation.

direction: clone the repository first, using the git module. For the task options, set update: yes and force: no; that way whenever the playbook is run, it will check for updates but skip the task if there are no updates. generate that task as well as the tasks to install virtual env and requirements

{
"query": "ti:ansible git module usage",
"max_results": 5
}

Based on the search results, the paper "Dozer: Migrating Shell Commands to Ansible Modules via Execution Profiling and Synthesis" seems to be the most relevant. It discusses a technique to help developers push their shell commands into Ansible task definitions. This could be useful for our task of cloning the repository using the git module in Ansible.
Here is the link to the paper.
Now, let's generate the tasks as directed.


Clone the repository using the git module in Ansible. We can set update: yes and force: no in the task options to check for updates but skip the task if there are no updates.
- name: Clone the repository git: repo: 'https://github.com/princeton-nlp/tree-of-thought-llm.git' dest: '{{ workspace }}' update: yes force: no Copy

Create a virtual environment.
- name: Create a virtual environment command: python3 -m venv venv args: chdir: '{{ workspace }}' register: venv failed_when: venv.rc != 0 Copy

Upgrade pip in the virtual environment.
- name: Upgrade pip pip: name: pip state: latest virtualenv: '{{ workspace }}/venv' Copy

Install requirements.
- name: Install requirements pip: requirements: '{{ workspace }}/requirements.txt' virtualenv: '{{ workspace }}/venv' Copy
These tasks should be placed within the pre_tasks block and run only if Ansible doesn't detect the venv is installed. We can use the stat module to check for the existence of the venv before running these tasks. ❗ |
| Critic 🧑‍🎨: | The tasks look good. However, we should also consider adding a task to activate the virtual environment before running the experiments. This can be done using the shell module in Ansible. ❗ |
| AntiSteve: | While the tasks are well-defined, I'm wondering if we should also consider the possibility of the requirements changing. Should we add a task to check for updates to the requirements file before installing the requirements? ❗ |
| Scrum Leader 🧓: | Good points. Let's add these tasks to our backlog. We'll need to discuss how to handle potential changes to the requirements and how to activate the virtual environment before running the experiments. ❗ |
Now, let's continue the discussion. Please use the available commands to guide the conversation.

direction: generate a task to activate the virtual env before experiments are run. AntiSteve: we'll determine the requirments


- name: Activate virtual environment shell: | source {{ workspace }}/venv/bin/activate Copy
This task uses the shell module to source the activate script in the virtual environment, which sets the necessary environment variables to use the virtual environment. ❗ |
| Critic 🧑‍🎨: | That's a good start. However, we should also consider the fact that the shell module in Ansible does not persist environment variables between tasks. This means that even though we activate the virtual environment in one task, the environment variables set by the activate script will not be available in subsequent tasks. We might need to source the activate script in every task that requires the virtual environment. ❗ |
| AntiSteve: | I see the value in activating the virtual environment before running the experiments. However, I'm wondering if we should also consider the possibility of the virtual environment not being set up correctly. Should we add a task to check the status of the virtual environment before trying to activate it? ❗ |
| Scrum Leader 🧓: | Good points. Let's add these tasks to our backlog. We'll need to discuss how to handle the activation of the virtual environment and how to check its status. ❗ |
Now, let's continue the discussion. Please use the available commands to guide the conversation.

suggestion: use a handler to activate the virtual env


handlers: - name: Activate virtual environment shell: | source {{ workspace }}/venv/bin/activate Copy
Then, in any task that requires the virtual environment, we can notify the handler like this:
- name: Some task command: some-command notify: Activate virtual environment Copy
This way, the virtual environment will be activated whenever it's needed. ❗ |
| Critic 🧑‍🎨: | That's a clever use of handlers. However, we should also remember that handlers are only run once, even if they are notified multiple times. This means that if we have multiple tasks that notify the "Activate virtual environment" handler, the virtual environment will only be activated once. We need to ensure that this is the behavior we want. ❗ |
| AntiSteve: | While I see the value in using handlers, I'm wondering if we should also consider the possibility of the activation of the virtual environment failing. Should we add a task to check the status of the virtual environment after trying to activate it? ❗ |
| Scrum Leader 🧓: | Good points. Let's add these tasks to our backlog. We'll need to discuss how to handle the activation of the virtual environment and how to check its status. ❗ |
Now, let's continue the discussion. Please use the available commands to guide the conversation.

input_topic: Designing an Ansible Playbook to automate the experiments from this repository: <https://github.com/princeton-nlp/tree-of-thought-llm>
num_thoughts: 2

Alright, let's start the discussion. The topic is "Designing an Ansible Playbook to automate the experiments from this repository: https://github.com/princeton-nlp/tree-of-thought-llm". We will be generating 2 thoughts at each state. The output format will be JSON.
Prompt Engineer 🧑‍🔬: As a starting point, we should first understand the structure of the experiments in the repository. We need to identify the steps that are repetitive and can be automated using Ansible. I'll take a look at the repository and come up with a basic structure for the playbook.
Critic 🧑‍🎨: I agree with the Prompt Engineer. Understanding the structure is crucial. However, we should also consider the dependencies of the experiments. The playbook should ensure that all the necessary software and libraries are installed and configured correctly.
AntiSteve: While I agree with both of you, I think we should also consider the possibility of errors during the execution of the playbook. We should design the playbook in such a way that it can handle errors gracefully and provide meaningful error messages.
Scrum Leader 🧓: Good points, everyone. Let's start by understanding the structure of the experiments and identifying the repetitive steps. We'll also consider the dependencies and error handling. Prompt Engineer, could you start by examining the repository?
❗

continue

Prompt Engineer 🧑‍🔬: After examining the repository, I've identified several steps that we can automate using Ansible. The experiments seem to follow a pattern of data preprocessing, model training, and result evaluation. We can create tasks in our playbook for each of these steps.
Critic 🧑‍🎨: That's a good start. However, we need to ensure that the tasks in our playbook are idempotent. This means that no matter how many times we run the playbook, the end state should be the same. For example, if a task is to install a software package, running the task multiple times should not result in multiple installations of the same package.
AntiSteve: I agree with the Critic. Idempotency is a key feature of Ansible and we should make full use of it. Additionally, we should also consider the possibility of failures during the execution of the playbook. We should design the playbook in such a way that it can handle failures gracefully and provide meaningful error messages.
Scrum Leader 🧓: Great points, everyone. Let's keep these considerations in mind as we design our playbook. Prompt Engineer, could you start by creating a task for data preprocessing?
❗

continue

Prompt Engineer 🧑‍🔬: Sure, I've started working on the data preprocessing task. I'm writing a script that will automate the process of downloading and preprocessing the data. This script will be called from our Ansible playbook.
Critic 🧑‍🎨: That's a good start. However, we should also consider the possibility of the data source being unavailable or the data format changing. We should add checks to ensure that the data is downloaded correctly and is in the expected format.
AntiSteve: I agree with the Critic. We should also consider the possibility of the data preprocessing script failing. We should add error handling to the script to ensure that it fails gracefully and provides a meaningful error message.
Scrum Leader 🧓: Good points, everyone. Let's add these checks and error handling to our data preprocessing task. Prompt Engineer, could you update the script accordingly?
❗

suggestion: wrap tasks within a begin/recuse block

Scrum Leader 🧓: That's a good suggestion. Wrapping tasks within a begin/rescue block will allow us to handle any exceptions that might occur during the execution of the tasks. This way, we can ensure that our playbook fails gracefully and provides meaningful error messages. Prompt Engineer, could you implement this in our playbook?
Prompt Engineer 🧑‍🔬: Absolutely, I'll wrap the tasks within a begin/rescue block. This will allow us to catch and handle any exceptions that might occur during the execution of the tasks.
Critic 🧑‍🎨: That's a good approach. However, we should also consider logging the exceptions. This will help us in debugging any issues that might occur during the execution of the playbook.
AntiSteve: I agree with the Critic. Logging exceptions is a good practice. We should also consider sending notifications in case of failures. This will allow us to quickly respond to any issues that might occur during the execution of the playbook.

Here are some relevant papers that might help us in our discussion:

Automated Code generation for Information Technology Tasks in YAML through Large Language Models by Saurabh Pujar et al. This paper presents Ansible Wisdom, a natural-language to Ansible-YAML code generation tool, aimed at improving IT automation productivity.
Cybersecurity Playbook Sharing with STIX 2.1 by Vasileios Mavroeidis and Mateusz Zych. This report documents the process of extending Structured Threat Information eXpression (STIX) version 2.1 to enable sharing security playbooks, including Collaborative Automated Course of Action Operations (CACAO) playbooks.
Autonomous Attack Mitigation for Industrial Control Systems by John Mern et al. This work presents a deep reinforcement learning approach to autonomous response and recovery in large industrial control networks.
Reviewing BPMN as a Modeling Notation for CACAO Security Playbooks by Mateusz Zych et al. This research examines BPMN and CACAO and explores the feasibility of using the BPMN modeling notation to graphically represent CACAO security playbooks.
GLITCH: Automated Polyglot Security Smell Detection in Infrastructure as Code by Nuno Saavedra and João F. Ferreira. This paper presents GLITCH, a new technology-agnostic framework that enables automated polyglot smell detection by transforming IaC scripts into an intermediate representation.
These papers provide insights into various aspects of Ansible playbook automation, including code generation, playbook sharing, autonomous attack mitigation, modeling notation for security playbooks, and automated polyglot security smell detection.
